mnemonics: FEFE: Fork Executes a copy, Fork returns twice, Exec replaces the image, Exit terminates the process.
keyPoints: A process is an instance of a running program with its own memory space and system resources.
The fork() system call creates a new (child) process, which is a duplicate of the calling (parent) process.
The exec() family of functions replaces the current process image with a new program.
The wait() and waitpid() system calls allow a parent process to suspend its execution until a child process terminates.
The exit() system call terminates a process, closing all file descriptors and releasing resources.
commonMistakes: Not checking the return value of fork(), leading to undefined behavior when the call fails.
Forgetting that after a successful fork(), both the parent and child execute the next instruction, causing unintended code execution.
Using exit() in a child process without properly communicating its status to the parent, creating a zombie process.
